text: This video demonstrates a case of laparoscopic splenectomy for a vascular splenic mass.
The patient is a thirty-two-year-old male who presented to the emergency department three days after blunt abdominal trauma with diffuse abdominal pain more in the right upper quadrant.
The patient has no significant past medical or past surgical history.
The physical exam was significant for tenderness to palpation over the right middle and inferior chest wall with mild tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant.
He had no left upper quadrant tenderness.
His labs were within normal limits.
His abdominal CT scan showed a splenic mass which was confirmed by MRI to be a vascular mass suggestive of a vascular tumor.
The patient was scheduled for an elective surgery and received immunizations against pneumococcus and Haemophilus influenzae type B two weeks before surgery.
The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position on the operating table. Four laparoscopic ports are visibly placed on the patient's abdomen.
The sequence of surgery was as indicated in this slide.
The surgeon achieves adequate exposure and frees the inferior pole of the spleen from adhesions, then ligates and divides the inferior polar vessels.

The surgeon frees the superior pole of the spleen by initially taking down the superior short gastric vessels, followed by the complete takedown of the remaining short gastric vessels and superior polar vessels.
The surgeon dissects the splenic hilum, isolating both the splenic artery and vein from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery with a magistral distribution, where the artery visibly divides near the hilum.
This happens in approximately thirty percent of the population.
The surgeon uses a vascular stapler device to individually transect each vessel at the splenic hilum, achieving good hemostasis.
The surgeon carefully performs dissection in the hilar area, ensuring that the pancreas remains uninjured during the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the spleen by carefully freeing its attachments, including the splenocolic, splenorenal, and splenophrenic ligaments, allowing for increased maneuverability of the organ.
During the maneuver, the splenic mass is brought into view and carefully protected from injury.
The surgeon places the freed spleen into an endobag and performs partial marsupialization before extracting it from the surgical field.
The surgical field is minimally irrigated, and hemostasis is visually confirmed, indicating no active bleeding.
The surgeon uses a zero-vicryl suture to close the fascia of the left subcostal incision, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The final pathology revealed organizing splenic hematoma.
In conclusion, laparoscopic splenectomy for vascular splenic mass can be performed safely.
Understanding the anatomical variation of the splenic artery distribution is an important aspect during this procedure to minimize the blood loss.
In a follow-up eight months after surgery, the patient is doing well.
